title: Parameria pedunculosa
intro: Parameria pedunculosa, also known as Himalayan sweetroot or Himalayan sweetroot plant, is a perennial plant species native to the Himalayas and other parts of Asia, characterized by its sweet-smelling, white flowers and edible roots. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may help alleviate digestive issues, reduce fever, and protect against infections. The bioactive constituents of Parameria pedunculosa include iridoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make various herbal preparations, including decoctions, infusions, and powders, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, cough, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Parameria pedunculosa is classified within the family Rubiaceae, commonly known as the coffee or madder family. This family is a large group of flowering plants that includes around 600 genera and over 13,000 species. The genus Parameria belongs to the tribe Spermacoceae, which is a subtribe of the Rubiaceae family. The classification of Parameria pedunculosa is further supported by its characteristic features and molecular analysis. Further research and classification may be subject to revision as new evidence emerges.
